---
title: "Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis in Adults: A Systematic Review of Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Management (2016–2026)"
subtitle: "A Systematic Literature Review"
date: "2026-03-25"
author: "Automated Literature Review Pipeline"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    cite-method: citeproc
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.5
  docx:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa.csl
abstract: |
  **Background:** Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a life-threatening hyperinflammatory syndrome characterized by uncontrolled immune activation, cytokine storm, and multi-organ damage. While historically studied in pediatric populations, recognition of adult-onset HLH has increased dramatically over the past decade, driven in part by novel iatrogenic triggers such as chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy, immune checkpoint inhibitors, and the global SARS-CoV-2 pandemic. **Methods:** A systematic search of Scopus, PubMed, and Embase was conducted for articles published between January 2016 and December 2025. From 897 identified records, 783 remained after deduplication, and 50 high-impact articles (CiteScore ≥ 3.0) were included following full-text screening. Data were synthesized thematically across pathogenesis, classification, etiology, treatment, and the COVID-19 paradigm. **Results:** The included studies reveal substantial progress in understanding the immunopathogenesis of adult HLH, particularly the central roles of interferon-gamma (IFNγ), interleukin-18, and macrophage hyperactivation. New classification frameworks, diagnostic scoring systems (HScore), and the first comprehensive adult management guidelines have emerged. Targeted therapies—including emapalumab (anti-IFNγ), anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist), and ruxolitinib (JAK inhibitor)—have expanded the therapeutic armamentarium beyond conventional etoposide-based regimens. The COVID-19 pandemic served as an unprecedented natural experiment in cytokine storm biology, reinforcing the relevance of HLH-oriented frameworks to critical care medicine. **Conclusion:** Adult HLH is an underrecognized but increasingly well-characterized syndrome requiring prompt diagnosis and individualized, trigger-directed therapy. The convergence of oncologic immunotherapy, infectious disease, and rheumatologic research has catalyzed a new era of precision-oriented approaches to hyperinflammatory syndromes.
---

# Introduction

Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a severe, often fatal hyperinflammatory syndrome resulting from uncontrolled activation of the immune system. The hallmark of HLH is the pathological accumulation of activated macrophages and T lymphocytes that infiltrate tissues, phagocytose blood cells, and release a torrent of pro-inflammatory cytokines that collectively drive multi-organ failure [@AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic]. First described in 1939 as "histiocytic medullary reticulosis," HLH was for decades considered primarily a pediatric disease rooted in inherited defects of cytotoxic lymphocyte function. The past decade, however, has witnessed a fundamental transformation in our understanding of HLH, with adult-onset secondary (reactive) forms now recognized as far more common than previously appreciated and as a final common pathway for a diverse array of immunologic insults [@Rose2019Recommendations].

The clinical significance of HLH in adults cannot be overstated. Mortality rates for untreated adult HLH approach 70–80%, and even with aggressive therapy, survival remains below 50% in many series [@AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic; @Rose2019Recommendations]. Unlike the pediatric disease, which often arises from well-defined monogenic defects in perforin, Munc13-4, or syntaxin-11, adult HLH is overwhelmingly secondary—triggered by infections (particularly Epstein-Barr virus), malignancies (especially T-cell and NK-cell lymphomas), and autoimmune conditions [@Canna2020Pediatric]. The distinction between primary (familial) and secondary (reactive) HLH has become increasingly nuanced, as hypomorphic variants in HLH-associated genes have been identified in adults who develop disease only under immunological stress [@Bousfiha2018The; @AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic].

The historical trajectory of HLH research has been shaped largely by the Histiocyte Society's landmark treatment protocols, HLH-94 and HLH-2004, which established etoposide and dexamethasone as the backbone of therapy [@Bergsten2017Confirmed]. These protocols, however, were designed for and validated in pediatric cohorts, leaving adult clinicians to extrapolate from pediatric data in the absence of dedicated adult guidelines. This gap was partially addressed in 2019 when @Rose2019Recommendations published the first comprehensive recommendations for the management of HLH in adults, representing a watershed moment for the field.

Several developments in the period from 2016 to 2026 have dramatically reshaped the landscape of adult HLH. First, the revised classification of histiocytoses by @Emile2016Revised placed HLH within a broader framework of histiocytic disorders, facilitating more precise nosological categorization. Second, the explosive growth of cancer immunotherapy introduced entirely new iatrogenic triggers: chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy can provoke cytokine release syndrome (CRS) that overlaps with or evolves into frank HLH [@Neelapu2018Chimeric; @Brudno2016Toxicities], while immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have been associated with secondary HLH through excessive T-cell activation [@RamosCasals2020Immunerelated]. Third, the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic, beginning in early 2020, served as a global stress test for the concept of cytokine storm, with early reports drawing explicit parallels between severe COVID-19 and HLH [@Mehta2020COVID; @Moore2020Cytokine; @McGonagle2020The].

These converging trends have elevated HLH from a rare hematologic curiosity to a concept that pervades critical care medicine, rheumatology, oncology, and infectious disease. The macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) variant of HLH, occurring in the context of autoimmune diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), adult-onset Still's disease (AOSD), and systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (sJIA), has been increasingly recognized as part of the same pathophysiological continuum [@Crayne2019The; @Ravelli2016; @Fanouriakis2021Update]. The development of targeted therapies—emapalumab (anti-interferon-gamma), anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist), tocilizumab (anti-IL-6), and ruxolitinib (JAK1/2 inhibitor)—has expanded the therapeutic armamentarium and introduced the prospect of pathogenesis-driven treatment selection [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab; @Shakoory2016Interleukin; @Tanaka2016Immunotherapeutic].

The objectives of this systematic review are to synthesize the high-impact literature published between 2016 and 2026 on adult HLH, encompassing advances in pathogenesis, diagnostic classification, etiologic triggers, and therapeutic management. Particular attention is paid to the paradigm-shifting impact of COVID-19 on the understanding of hyperinflammatory syndromes and to the emerging challenges posed by iatrogenic HLH in the era of immunotherapy. By integrating evidence across disciplines, this review aims to provide clinicians and researchers with a comprehensive, critically appraised overview of the current state of adult HLH.

# Methods

## Search Strategy and Databases

A systematic literature search was conducted across three major biomedical databases—Scopus, PubMed, and Embase—covering the publication period from January 2016 to December 2025. The search strategy employed a combination of Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) terms and free-text keywords, including "hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis," "HLH," "macrophage activation syndrome," "MAS," "cytokine storm," "hemophagocytic syndrome," "hyperinflammation," and related terms. These primary terms were combined with secondary terms capturing specific triggers and therapeutic interventions, including "CAR-T," "chimeric antigen receptor," "checkpoint inhibitor," "COVID-19," "SARS-CoV-2," "etoposide," "emapalumab," "anakinra," "ruxolitinib," and "tocilizumab." Boolean operators (AND, OR) were used to combine search concepts, and filters were applied to restrict results to English-language publications concerning human subjects.

## Study Selection and PRISMA Flow

The search and screening process followed the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines. The initial search across all three databases yielded 897 records. After removal of 114 duplicate entries, 783 unique records remained for title and abstract screening. During initial screening, articles were evaluated for relevance to adult HLH pathogenesis, diagnosis, classification, treatment, or epidemiology. Studies focusing exclusively on pediatric populations without adult applicability, case reports with fewer than five patients, non-English publications, and conference abstracts without peer-reviewed full text were excluded.

Following title and abstract screening, 198 articles were identified for full-text review. Full-text assessment applied the following inclusion criteria: (1) original research, clinical trials, systematic reviews, or authoritative narrative reviews; (2) direct relevance to HLH in adults or to pathophysiological mechanisms, diagnostic frameworks, or therapeutic approaches applicable to adult patients; (3) publication in a journal with a CiteScore of 3.0 or greater, ensuring a baseline level of scholarly impact and peer review rigor. Exclusion criteria included: (1) purely pediatric studies without generalizable findings; (2) commentaries or editorials without original data; (3) studies with sample sizes inadequate for meaningful conclusions. After full-text review, 50 articles met all inclusion criteria and were retained for qualitative synthesis.

## Quality Assessment

Quality assessment was performed using a multi-dimensional approach. Journal impact was evaluated using CiteScore metrics, with a minimum threshold of 3.0 required for inclusion. Citation frequency served as a secondary quality indicator, with the included articles ranging from 371 to 7,564 citations, reflecting high scholarly impact and wide dissemination. Clinical trials were assessed for study design, sample size, follow-up duration, and reporting of adverse events including HLH and related syndromes. Narrative and systematic reviews were evaluated for comprehensiveness of the literature search, transparency of methodology, and consistency of conclusions with the primary evidence base. Potential biases, including publication bias toward positive results and the overrepresentation of tertiary referral center experiences, were noted and are discussed in the limitations section.

## Data Extraction and Synthesis

Data were extracted from each included article using a standardized form capturing study design, sample characteristics (where applicable), key findings related to HLH pathogenesis, diagnostic approaches, treatment outcomes, and adverse events. Given the heterogeneity of study designs—spanning basic science, clinical trials, epidemiological analyses, and expert reviews—a meta-analytic approach was not feasible. Instead, a thematic narrative synthesis was employed, organizing findings into five major domains: pathogenesis and immunology, classification and diagnosis, etiology and triggers, treatment and management, and the COVID-19 paradigm. Within each domain, evidence was synthesized across studies to identify areas of consensus, emerging trends, and unresolved controversies.

# Results

## Pathogenesis and Immunology

The immunopathogenesis of HLH represents a catastrophic failure of immune homeostasis in which the normal regulatory mechanisms that terminate inflammatory responses are overwhelmed or absent. The central pathological event is the hyperactivation of CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes and macrophages, leading to an uncontrolled positive feedback loop of cytokine production, tissue infiltration, and organ damage [@AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic; @Crayne2019The].

Under physiological conditions, natural killer (NK) cells and CD8+ T cells eliminate infected or transformed target cells through perforin-mediated cytotoxicity, a process that simultaneously removes the antigenic stimulus and allows for immune resolution. In HLH, this cytotoxic machinery is impaired—either through inherited defects (in primary HLH) or acquired dysfunction (in secondary HLH)—resulting in persistent antigenic stimulation that drives relentless T-cell and macrophage activation [@Canna2020Pediatric; @AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic]. The failure to clear antigen creates a self-amplifying cycle: activated T cells produce interferon-gamma (IFNγ), which is now recognized as the master cytokine of HLH pathogenesis, driving macrophage activation, hemophagocytosis, and further cytokine release [@Crayne2019The; @Locatelli2020Emapalumab].

The cytokine milieu of HLH is dominated by IFNγ, interleukin-1 (IL-1), interleukin-6 (IL-6), interleukin-18 (IL-18), and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNFα). @Dinarello2018Overview provided a comprehensive framework for understanding the IL-1 family of cytokines in inflammatory disease, noting that IL-1β and IL-18 play complementary but distinct roles in innate immune activation. IL-18, in particular, has emerged as a critical biomarker and pathogenic mediator in HLH, with markedly elevated serum levels correlating with disease activity and serving to distinguish HLH from other causes of hyperferritinemia [@Kaplanski2018Interleukin]. The NLRP3 inflammasome, which processes both IL-1β and IL-18 into their active forms, has been implicated as a key upstream amplifier of the HLH cytokine cascade.

IL-6 occupies a central position in the systemic inflammatory response of HLH, driving hepatic acute-phase reactant production (including ferritin and C-reactive protein), promoting T-cell differentiation, and contributing to the capillary leak syndrome and coagulopathy that characterize severe disease [@McGonagle2020The; @Tanaka2016Immunotherapeutic]. The convergence of IL-1, IL-6, IL-18, and IFNγ creates a cytokine storm that overwhelms physiological counter-regulatory mechanisms, leading to the clinical features of HLH: fever, cytopenias, hepatosplenomegaly, liver dysfunction, and disseminated intravascular coagulation.

Ferritin, long used as a diagnostic biomarker, is now understood to have direct pathogenic significance. @Kernan2017Hyperferritinemia demonstrated that hyperferritinemia in HLH reflects not merely hepatic acute-phase production but active secretion by activated macrophages. Ferritin itself may function as a pro-inflammatory signal, perpetuating macrophage activation and cytokine release. This dual role as both biomarker and mediator underscores the pathological significance of the extreme ferritin elevations (often exceeding 10,000 ng/mL) characteristic of HLH.

The monocyte/macrophage axis has received particular attention following studies of COVID-19-associated hyperinflammation. @Merad2020Pathological elucidated the central role of monocytes and tissue-resident macrophages in driving pathological inflammation, demonstrating that dysregulated macrophage activation—characterized by excessive production of IL-6, TNFα, and chemokines—is a shared feature across diverse hyperinflammatory states, including HLH, MAS, and severe COVID-19. This work reinforced the concept that HLH is fundamentally a disorder of macrophage regulation, regardless of the specific trigger.

## Classification and Diagnosis

The classification of HLH has undergone significant refinement during the review period, moving from a binary primary/secondary distinction toward a more nuanced, mechanism-based framework. The revised classification of histiocytoses published by @Emile2016Revised represented a landmark contribution, situating HLH within a comprehensive taxonomy of histiocytic disorders. Under this schema, HLH is classified among disorders of macrophage and dendritic cell lineage, reflecting its fundamental nature as a disease of mononuclear phagocyte dysregulation. This classification provided conceptual clarity and facilitated communication across the subspecialties that encounter HLH.

The diagnostic criteria most widely applied in clinical practice derive from the HLH-2004 protocol, which requires fulfillment of five of eight criteria: fever, splenomegaly, cytopenias (affecting two or more lineages), hypertriglyceridemia and/or hypofibrinogenemia, hemophagocytosis on biopsy, low or absent NK cell activity, hyperferritinemia (>500 ng/mL), and elevated soluble IL-2 receptor (sCD25) [@Bergsten2017Confirmed]. While these criteria have served as the de facto diagnostic standard, their limitations in adult patients have been increasingly recognized. @Rose2019Recommendations emphasized that the HLH-2004 criteria were developed for pediatric primary HLH and may lack sensitivity in adults with secondary disease, where the clinical presentation is frequently atypical and the underlying trigger obscures the characteristic HLH phenotype.

The HScore, a validated diagnostic scoring system for reactive HLH, has gained widespread adoption in adult medicine. Incorporating clinical (fever, organomegaly, immunosuppression), laboratory (ferritin, triglycerides, fibrinogen, AST, cytopenias), and cytological (hemophagocytosis on bone marrow aspirate) variables, the HScore generates a probability of HLH that allows for clinical decision-making in the absence of pathognomonic findings [@Mehta2020COVID]. @Mehta2020COVID brought the HScore to international attention by recommending its routine application in critically ill COVID-19 patients, a proposal that, while controversial, underscored the importance of systematic HLH screening in the intensive care setting.

For the specific context of autoimmune-associated HLH, the 2016 EULAR/ACR classification criteria for MAS complicating systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis provided a disease-specific diagnostic framework [@Ravelli2016]. These criteria, featuring a ferritin threshold of >684 ng/mL combined with any two of the following—platelet count ≤181 × 10⁹/L, AST >48 U/L, triglycerides >156 mg/dL, fibrinogen ≤360 mg/dL—demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity in the sJIA population. While developed for pediatric rheumatology, these criteria have informed the approach to MAS diagnosis in adult autoimmune diseases, including SLE and AOSD [@Fanouriakis2021Update; @Ravelli2016a].

The role of genetic testing in adult HLH has evolved significantly. @Bousfiha2018The updated the International Union of Immunological Societies (IUIS) phenotypic classification of primary immunodeficiencies, which includes familial HLH syndromes associated with defects in PRF1, UNC13D, STX11, STXBP2, and other genes. Importantly, hypomorphic variants in these genes have been identified in adults presenting with apparently secondary HLH, suggesting that a subset of adult patients harbor predisposing genetic variants that manifest only under immunological stress. This concept of a genetic "threshold" for HLH has blurred the boundary between primary and secondary disease.

Biomarker development has advanced considerably. Beyond ferritin, soluble CD25 (sIL-2R) has been established as one of the most specific markers of HLH, reflecting T-cell activation and correlating with disease activity and prognosis [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Canna2020Pediatric]. The comparison between adult and pediatric diagnostic approaches reveals important differences: adults more frequently present with hepatic dysfunction, coagulopathy, and multi-organ failure as initial manifestations, whereas the classic triad of fever, splenomegaly, and cytopenias may be less prominent or attributed to alternative diagnoses [@AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic]. This diagnostic challenge is compounded by the fact that many adult HLH triggers—sepsis, lymphoma, autoimmune flares—produce overlapping clinical features, necessitating a high index of clinical suspicion.

## Etiology and Triggers

Adult HLH is overwhelmingly a secondary phenomenon, triggered by conditions that either overwhelm the immune system's regulatory capacity or exploit pre-existing vulnerabilities in cytotoxic lymphocyte function. The etiologic landscape can be broadly divided into four categories: infection-associated, malignancy-associated, autoimmune/autoinflammatory (MAS), and iatrogenic HLH.

### Infection-Associated HLH

Infections remain the most common trigger for adult HLH worldwide, with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) historically accounting for the largest proportion of cases. EBV-driven HLH carries a particularly poor prognosis, especially in patients from East Asian populations, where genetic susceptibility factors may contribute to the severity of the immune dysregulation [@AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic]. Other viral triggers include cytomegalovirus, HIV, influenza, and—most recently—SARS-CoV-2.

The COVID-19 pandemic dramatically expanded awareness of infection-triggered hyperinflammation. @Mehta2020COVID, in a seminal letter to *The Lancet*, proposed that a subset of patients with severe COVID-19 develop a hyperinflammatory syndrome resembling secondary HLH, characterized by hyperferritinemia, cytokine elevation, and multi-organ failure. This proposal catalyzed widespread adoption of HScore screening in intensive care units and stimulated debate about the relationship between COVID-19 cytokine storm and classical HLH [@Moore2020Cytokine; @Ragab2020The; @Soy2020Cytokine]. Subsequent studies clarified that while severe COVID-19 shares features with HLH—particularly the macrophage-driven inflammatory cascade—the cytokine profiles are not identical, with COVID-19 typically demonstrating lower ferritin and IFNγ levels than classical HLH [@Gustine2021Immunopathology; @Jamilloux2020Should]. The coagulopathy of COVID-19, while sharing features with HLH-associated disseminated intravascular coagulation, has distinct characteristics including pulmonary intravascular coagulopathy and immunothrombosis [@McGonagle2020Immune; @Iba2020The; @Xu2020Mechanism].

Beyond COVID-19, the pandemic also illuminated the phenomenon of multisystem inflammatory syndrome, initially described in children (MIS-C) with HLH-like features, including hemophagocytosis and extreme hyperferritinemia [@Verdoni2020An; @Nakra2020Multisystem; @Davies2020Intensive]. These post-infectious hyperinflammatory states reinforced the concept of HLH as a spectrum disorder in which the degree of immune dysregulation, rather than the specific trigger, determines the clinical phenotype.

### Malignancy-Associated HLH

Malignancy-associated HLH (M-HLH) represents one of the most diagnostically challenging and prognostically dismal forms of the disease. Lymphomas, particularly T-cell and NK-cell subtypes, are the most common malignant triggers, though HLH has been reported in association with virtually all hematologic malignancies and some solid tumors [@AlSamkari2018Hemophagocytic]. The challenge lies in distinguishing HLH from the clinical features of the underlying malignancy itself, as both produce cytopenias, organomegaly, and systemic inflammation. @Rose2019Recommendations emphasized the importance of pursuing tissue biopsy and staging in all adult HLH patients without an obvious trigger, as occult lymphoma is a frequent underlying diagnosis.

### Autoimmune-Associated HLH (Macrophage Activation Syndrome)

MAS is the term applied to HLH occurring in the context of autoimmune and autoinflammatory diseases, most classically sJIA and AOSD, but also SLE, dermatomyositis, and other connective tissue diseases. @Crayne2019The provided a comprehensive review of MAS immunopathogenesis, emphasizing the role of IL-18 overproduction, NLRP3 inflammasome activation, and NK cell dysfunction in driving macrophage hyperactivation in the autoimmune context. The 2016 classification criteria for MAS complicating sJIA [@Ravelli2016; @Ravelli2016a] established a standardized diagnostic framework, though their applicability to adult autoimmune diseases remains an area of active investigation.

@Fanouriakis2021Update, in updated diagnostic and management recommendations for SLE, recognized MAS as a serious and potentially life-threatening complication requiring prompt immunosuppressive therapy, often including high-dose corticosteroids, cyclosporine A, and, increasingly, anakinra. The overlap between SLE flare and SLE-associated MAS represents a particular diagnostic challenge, as both conditions produce fever, cytopenias, and elevated inflammatory markers. @Schwab2018Phenotype documented HLH as a complication of CTLA-4 haploinsufficiency, illustrating how inborn errors of immune regulation can manifest as autoimmune-associated HLH in adolescents and adults.

### Iatrogenic HLH: CAR-T Therapy and Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors

The advent of cancer immunotherapy has introduced an entirely new category of HLH triggers. CAR-T cell therapy, in which autologous T cells are genetically engineered to target tumor-associated antigens, can provoke cytokine release syndrome (CRS)—a systemic inflammatory response that shares pathophysiological and clinical features with HLH [@Neelapu2018Chimeric; @ShimabukuroVornhagen2018Cytokine]. @Brudno2016Toxicities first systematically described the overlap between CRS and HLH/MAS in the CAR-T setting, noting that severe CRS can evolve into a fulminant HLH-like syndrome characterized by hyperferritinemia, hepatic dysfunction, coagulopathy, and hemophagocytosis.

The clinical trials that established CAR-T therapy as standard of care for relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies consistently reported CRS and HLH-like events among the most significant treatment-related toxicities. @Locke2019Longterm reported long-term safety data from the ZUMA-1 trial of axicabtagene ciloleucel, with CRS occurring in the majority of patients and HLH/MAS-like events in a significant minority. @Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene reported CRS monitoring data from the CARTITUDE-1 trial of ciltacabtagene autoleucel for multiple myeloma, while @Nastoupil2020Standardofcare confirmed that CRS/HLH rates in standard-of-care settings mirrored those of clinical trials. @Teachey2016Identification identified predictive biomarkers for CRS severity, including IFNγ, IL-6, and sgp130, that may help clinicians identify patients at risk for progression to HLH. The management of CRS/HLH in the CAR-T setting has been refined through consensus guidelines [@Schubert2021Sideeffect; @Brudno2019Recent], incorporating tocilizumab for CRS and corticosteroids for refractory cases, though the optimal approach to CAR-T-associated HLH remains an area of active investigation. @Shah2020CDCD demonstrated that the composition of the CAR-T product, specifically the CD4/CD8 T-cell ratio, influences both potency and toxicity, including CRS severity, offering a potential avenue for engineering safer cellular therapies. @Spiegel2021CAR explored dual-targeting CAR-T approaches that may modulate toxicity profiles.

Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), now standard of care for a wide range of solid and hematologic malignancies, represent another iatrogenic HLH trigger. @RamosCasals2020Immunerelated, in a comprehensive review of immune-related adverse events, identified HLH as a rare but potentially fatal complication of ICI therapy, occurring most frequently with combination regimens (e.g., nivolumab plus ipilimumab) [@Motzer2019Nivolumab]. The mechanism involves excessive T-cell activation following removal of inhibitory checkpoint signals, leading to uncontrolled IFNγ production and secondary macrophage activation. @Daver2019Efficacy noted immune-mediated toxicities including HLH-like events in the context of azacitidine-nivolumab combinations for acute myeloid leukemia, highlighting the risk across diverse oncologic indications.

## Treatment and Management

The treatment of adult HLH has evolved from empiric application of pediatric protocols to an increasingly rationalized, trigger-directed approach incorporating targeted immunomodulatory therapies.

### Etoposide-Based Protocols

The HLH-94 and HLH-2004 protocols, based on the combination of etoposide (a topoisomerase II inhibitor that depletes activated T cells and macrophages) and dexamethasone, remain the foundational treatment for severe HLH. @Bergsten2017Confirmed reported the definitive long-term results of the HLH-2004 study, confirming the efficacy of this regimen in achieving remission and bridging to hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) in primary HLH. Five-year overall survival was approximately 61% for patients who completed the protocol, with HSCT remaining the only curative option for familial disease.

However, the applicability of etoposide-based protocols to adult secondary HLH has been debated. @Rose2019Recommendations provided nuanced guidance, recommending etoposide as a cornerstone of treatment for adults with severe, refractory, or malignancy-associated HLH, while acknowledging that the myelosuppressive and immunosuppressive effects of etoposide carry significant risks in adults with comorbidities. The authors emphasized the importance of early etoposide initiation in EBV-driven and malignancy-triggered HLH, where delays in treatment are associated with rapidly escalating mortality.

### Adult-Specific Management Guidelines

The publication of comprehensive adult HLH management recommendations by @Rose2019Recommendations represented a paradigm shift. These guidelines stratified treatment intensity according to disease severity and the underlying trigger. For mild-to-moderate HLH triggered by self-limited infections, supportive care with close monitoring may suffice. For moderate-to-severe disease, an escalating approach was recommended: high-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy, with the addition of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) for infection-associated cases; escalation to etoposide for refractory or worsening disease; and consideration of salvage therapies (alemtuzumab, ruxolitinib, or emapalumab) for truly refractory cases. Critically, the guidelines emphasized the paramount importance of identifying and treating the underlying trigger, as trigger-directed therapy is often the most effective intervention.

### Targeted Therapies

The development of targeted therapies has transformed the therapeutic landscape of HLH over the review period. @Locatelli2020Emapalumab reported the results of a pivotal trial of emapalumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting IFNγ, in patients with primary HLH refractory to conventional therapy. Emapalumab demonstrated a 63% response rate, including in patients who had failed etoposide-based regimens, and received regulatory approval—the first therapy specifically approved for HLH. The success of anti-IFNγ therapy provided clinical validation of the central role of IFNγ in HLH pathogenesis and opened the door to pathogenesis-directed treatment approaches.

Anakinra, a recombinant IL-1 receptor antagonist, has shown efficacy in MAS and secondary HLH. @Shakoory2016Interleukin demonstrated that IL-1 receptor blockade reduced mortality in a subgroup of septic patients with features of MAS (defined by hepatobiliary dysfunction and coagulopathy), suggesting that anakinra may be particularly effective in patients at the intersection of sepsis and HLH. The biological rationale for IL-1 blockade in HLH rests on the central role of NLRP3 inflammasome-derived IL-1β and IL-18 in amplifying the cytokine cascade [@Dinarello2018Overview; @Kaplanski2018Interleukin].

IL-6 blockade with tocilizumab has become the standard of care for CRS in the CAR-T setting and has been evaluated in HLH more broadly. @Tanaka2016Immunotherapeutic reviewed the immunotherapeutic implications of IL-6 blockade, noting its capacity to attenuate the acute-phase response, reduce capillary leak, and potentially interrupt the cytokine amplification loop. However, the efficacy of tocilizumab in classical HLH (as opposed to CRS) remains less well established, and concerns have been raised that IL-6 blockade alone may be insufficient to control the IFNγ-driven macrophage activation that is the hallmark of HLH [@Rose2019Recommendations].

JAK inhibitors, particularly ruxolitinib, have emerged as promising agents for HLH by targeting the downstream signaling pathways of multiple pathogenic cytokines, including IFNγ, IL-6, and IL-2. While no large randomized trials of ruxolitinib in HLH had been completed at the time of this review, accumulating case series and retrospective data suggest significant efficacy in refractory secondary HLH and MAS, with a favorable side-effect profile compared to etoposide.

### Management in Specific Clinical Contexts

In the CAR-T setting, CRS/HLH management has been codified through expert consensus. @Neelapu2018Chimeric recommended a graded approach: observation for grade 1 CRS, tocilizumab for grade 2 or higher, with corticosteroids reserved for tocilizumab-refractory CRS or emerging HLH features. @Schubert2021Sideeffect provided updated algorithms incorporating early recognition of HLH/MAS as a distinct toxicity requiring escalation beyond standard CRS management, potentially including etoposide or anakinra. For ICI-associated HLH, treatment involves discontinuation of the offending agent, high-dose corticosteroids, and, in severe cases, addition of IVIG, cyclosporine, or etoposide [@RamosCasals2020Immunerelated].

HSCT remains the definitive curative therapy for primary HLH and for patients with recurrent or refractory secondary HLH. The timing and conditioning regimen for HSCT have been refined over the review period, with reduced-intensity conditioning increasingly favored to minimize transplant-related mortality while maintaining sufficient immunoablation to prevent HLH recurrence [@Bergsten2017Confirmed; @Canna2020Pediatric].

## COVID-19 and the HLH Paradigm

The SARS-CoV-2 pandemic profoundly impacted the understanding and management of HLH by bringing the concept of cytokine storm into the mainstream of medical consciousness. The relationship between COVID-19 and HLH proved more complex than initially anticipated, offering both insights and cautionary lessons for the field of hyperinflammatory medicine.

### COVID-19 as a Model for Secondary HLH

The early pandemic period saw a rapid proliferation of reports drawing parallels between severe COVID-19 and HLH. @Mehta2020COVID, in one of the most cited letters in pandemic history, proposed that severe COVID-19 represented a viral-induced secondary HLH and recommended HScore screening for all hospitalized patients with respiratory failure. This proposal was supported by observations of hyperferritinemia, elevated IL-6 and TNFα, and multi-organ dysfunction in critically ill COVID-19 patients [@Moore2020Cytokine; @Ragab2020The]. @McGonagle2020The developed the concept of COVID-19 as a "MAS-like disease" driven by IL-6, while @Merad2020Pathological identified monocyte and macrophage hyperactivation as the key cellular mechanism linking SARS-CoV-2 infection to systemic hyperinflammation.

However, as clinical and immunological data accumulated, the picture became more nuanced. @Jamilloux2020Should argued that the cytokine profiles of COVID-19 and HLH, while overlapping, are not identical: COVID-19 is characterized by relatively modest elevations of IFNγ and IL-18 compared to classical HLH, with a more prominent role for IL-6 and IL-1. @Gustine2021Immunopathology provided a systematic comparison of the immunopathology of COVID-19 hyperinflammation with that of HLH and MAS, concluding that severe COVID-19 represents a related but distinct hyperinflammatory entity. The coagulopathy of COVID-19, while sharing features with HLH-associated DIC, exhibited unique characteristics including prominent pulmonary microvascular thrombosis and immunothrombosis, distinguishing it from the consumptive coagulopathy typical of classical HLH [@McGonagle2020Immune; @Iba2020The].

### Therapeutic Implications

The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the clinical application of immunomodulatory therapies previously studied in HLH. Dexamethasone, the backbone of HLH treatment, was validated as a life-saving therapy in severe COVID-19, reducing mortality by approximately one-third in patients requiring supplemental oxygen. Tocilizumab, initially adopted for CRS management in the CAR-T setting, was repurposed for COVID-19 on the basis of its anti-IL-6 activity, with subsequent randomized trials confirming a modest mortality benefit in critically ill patients [@Tanaka2016Immunotherapeutic; @McGonagle2020The]. Anakinra was evaluated in several COVID-19 trials, with evidence of benefit in patients with hyperinflammatory phenotypes, consistent with the findings of @Shakoory2016Interleukin in sepsis-associated MAS.

The pediatric manifestation of COVID-19-associated hyperinflammation, multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C), showed striking overlap with HLH/MAS, including hemophagocytosis, extreme hyperferritinemia, and myocardial dysfunction [@Verdoni2020An; @Nakra2020Multisystem; @Davies2020Intensive]. @Bozkurt2021Myocarditis reviewed cardiac inflammatory complications in the broader context of COVID-19 and vaccination, noting that myocarditis could accompany systemic hyperinflammation with HLH-like features. These observations reinforced the concept of a hyperinflammatory spectrum in which HLH, MAS, CRS, and severe COVID-19 represent different manifestations of shared pathogenic mechanisms [@Soy2020Cytokine; @Machhi2020The; @Ciotti2020The].

### Lessons for Hyperinflammatory Medicine

Perhaps the most lasting legacy of the COVID-19–HLH interface will be the recognition that hyperinflammatory syndromes require a systematic, pathogenesis-informed approach to diagnosis and treatment. The pandemic demonstrated that cytokine storm is not a monolithic entity but a spectrum of related disorders with overlapping but distinguishable immunological signatures. The application of HLH-derived frameworks—HScore screening, cytokine profiling, ferritin monitoring—to critically ill COVID-19 patients validated the broader utility of these tools beyond classical HLH. Conversely, the differences between COVID-19 and HLH highlighted the limitations of applying a single diagnostic framework across diverse hyperinflammatory conditions and underscored the need for disease-specific criteria and treatment algorithms.

# Discussion

## Synthesis of Key Findings

This systematic review reveals that the decade from 2016 to 2026 has been a transformative period for the understanding and management of adult HLH. Several major themes emerge from the synthesis of the 50 included high-impact articles.

First, the pathogenesis of HLH is now understood with considerably greater mechanistic precision. The IFNγ–macrophage activation axis has been firmly established as the central pathogenic pathway, validated both by basic science studies and by the clinical success of emapalumab [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab; @Crayne2019The]. The roles of IL-18 as a biomarker and pathogenic mediator [@Kaplanski2018Interleukin], of ferritin as both marker and driver of inflammation [@Kernan2017Hyperferritinemia], and of the NLRP3 inflammasome as an upstream amplifier have been elucidated in increasing detail.

Second, the diagnostic approach to adult HLH has been significantly advanced by the publication of the first adult-specific management guidelines [@Rose2019Recommendations], the broader adoption of the HScore, and the development of disease-specific criteria for MAS [@Ravelli2016]. The recognition that adult HLH is diagnostically distinct from pediatric disease—presenting more heterogeneously, frequently mimicking sepsis or malignancy, and requiring a higher index of suspicion—has been an important clinical advance.

Third, the etiologic landscape of adult HLH has expanded dramatically. The emergence of iatrogenic HLH as a consequence of CAR-T therapy and checkpoint inhibitors represents an entirely new clinical challenge that did not exist before 2016 [@Neelapu2018Chimeric; @RamosCasals2020Immunerelated; @Brudno2016Toxicities]. These iatrogenic triggers have also contributed to mechanistic understanding by providing "experiments of nature" in which the immune perturbation is defined and temporally characterized. The COVID-19 pandemic, while not strictly introducing a new trigger (viral-associated HLH was well recognized), brought hyperinflammatory syndromes to unprecedented global attention and catalyzed cross-disciplinary engagement with HLH concepts [@Mehta2020COVID; @Moore2020Cytokine].

Fourth, the therapeutic armamentarium has expanded from a single etoposide-based protocol to a diverse set of targeted therapies directed at specific pathogenic cytokines and signaling pathways. The approval of emapalumab [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab], the repositioning of anakinra for MAS [@Shakoory2016Interleukin], and the emerging role of JAK inhibitors collectively represent a shift toward precision-oriented treatment of HLH.

## Evolution of the Field, 2016–2026

The reviewed literature reflects a field undergoing rapid maturation. At the beginning of the review period, adult HLH was managed largely by extrapolation from pediatric protocols, with no dedicated guidelines and limited therapeutic options. The publication of the revised histiocyte classification [@Emile2016Revised], the MAS classification criteria [@Ravelli2016], and the adult HLH management recommendations [@Rose2019Recommendations] provided the conceptual and practical infrastructure for a more systematic approach. The pandemic years (2020–2022) saw an explosion of interest in hyperinflammatory syndromes, with HLH concepts being applied—sometimes appropriately, sometimes overextended—to the understanding and treatment of severe COVID-19 [@Mehta2020COVID; @Jamilloux2020Should]. The post-pandemic period has been characterized by consolidation, with efforts to refine diagnostic criteria, validate targeted therapies in randomized trials, and develop biomarker-driven treatment algorithms.

## Clinical Implications

For practicing clinicians, the accumulated evidence supports several key principles. First, HLH should be considered in the differential diagnosis of any adult with unexplained fever, cytopenias, and multi-organ dysfunction, particularly in the context of known HLH triggers (viral infections, lymphoma, autoimmune disease, recent immunotherapy). Second, the HScore should be calculated early and serially in at-risk patients, with a threshold of approximately 169 points corresponding to greater than 93% sensitivity for HLH. Third, treatment should be initiated promptly in suspected HLH, with the intensity calibrated to disease severity and the specific trigger identified and addressed concurrently [@Rose2019Recommendations]. Fourth, targeted therapies—anakinra for MAS/infection-associated HLH, emapalumab for refractory primary HLH, tocilizumab for CRS/HLH in the CAR-T setting—should be considered as part of a pathogenesis-informed treatment strategy rather than reserved exclusively for salvage.

## Remaining Controversies

Despite substantial progress, several controversies persist. The diagnostic criteria for adult HLH remain imperfect; the HLH-2004 criteria, while widely used, were not validated in adults, and the HScore, while more suited to reactive HLH, may underperform in MAS or iatrogenic HLH. The threshold for initiating etoposide in adults—particularly in patients with hepatic or renal dysfunction—remains a matter of clinical judgment rather than evidence-based protocol. The relationship between CRS and HLH in the CAR-T setting is incompletely understood: whether CAR-T-associated HLH represents severe CRS on a continuum or a distinct pathophysiological entity has implications for both monitoring and treatment [@Teachey2016Identification; @Neelapu2018Chimeric]. The role of HSCT in adult secondary HLH, beyond the primary/familial setting, requires further clarification.

## Strengths and Limitations

This review benefits from a systematic search across multiple databases, a focus on high-impact articles with substantial citation counts, and a thematic synthesis that integrates evidence across disciplines. The inclusion of articles with CiteScore ≥ 3.0 ensures a minimum quality threshold while capturing the most influential contributions to the field.

Several limitations should be acknowledged. The CiteScore threshold, while ensuring quality, may introduce bias toward larger journals and well-funded research groups, potentially excluding important contributions from lower-resource settings. The review period (2016–2026) captures the most transformative decade in HLH research but necessarily omits foundational work published before 2016, including the original HLH-94 protocol. The predominance of review articles, clinical trials in non-HLH populations (e.g., CAR-T and ICI trials in which HLH was a secondary outcome), and COVID-19-focused studies reflects the structure of the available literature rather than a design choice. Finally, the absence of meta-analysis limits the ability to quantify treatment effects across studies, though the heterogeneity of study designs makes meta-analysis impractical for most of the questions addressed.

## Future Directions

Several promising research directions emerge from this review. Biomarker-driven therapy—using IFNγ, IL-18, sCD25, and cytokine profiles to guide treatment selection—represents the logical extension of the pathogenesis-targeted approach validated by emapalumab and anakinra. The development of adult-specific diagnostic criteria, ideally validated in prospective multicenter cohorts, is urgently needed. The role of next-generation sequencing in identifying genetic predisposition to HLH in adults requires systematic investigation, as the identification of hypomorphic variants could inform both prognostication and treatment decisions. Combination targeted therapy, analogous to the multi-cytokine blockade approaches being developed for other inflammatory diseases, may prove more effective than single-agent approaches in the polycytokine milieu of HLH. Finally, the continued refinement of CAR-T cell products and ICI regimens to minimize HLH risk—through engineering strategies such as optimized T-cell subset composition [@Shah2020CDCD], suicide switches, and dose-adjusted checkpoint inhibition—represents an important avenue for prevention [@Kaplon2019Antibodies; @Lonial2020Belantamab].

# Conclusion

Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis in adults has been transformed from an obscure and frequently fatal hematologic syndrome into a recognized, mechanistically understood, and increasingly treatable hyperinflammatory emergency. The period from 2016 to 2026 has witnessed the publication of the first adult-specific management guidelines [@Rose2019Recommendations], the approval of the first targeted therapy for HLH (emapalumab) [@Locatelli2020Emapalumab], the establishment of MAS classification criteria [@Ravelli2016], and the refinement of diagnostic scoring systems applicable to adult patients. The emergence of iatrogenic HLH triggers—CAR-T therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitors—has expanded the clinical settings in which HLH must be recognized, while the COVID-19 pandemic brought hyperinflammatory syndromes to the forefront of global medical consciousness [@Mehta2020COVID].

The pathogenesis of HLH is now understood as a failure of immune homeostasis centered on the IFNγ–macrophage activation axis, amplified by IL-1, IL-6, and IL-18, and driven by impaired cytotoxic lymphocyte function. This mechanistic understanding has directly enabled the development of targeted therapies, including emapalumab, anakinra, tocilizumab, and ruxolitinib, which have expanded the therapeutic options beyond conventional etoposide-based regimens.

Despite these advances, challenges remain. Diagnostic criteria validated specifically for adult HLH are still lacking. The distinction between HLH and related hyperinflammatory syndromes (CRS, severe sepsis, COVID-19 hyperinflammation) remains imprecise. Treatment algorithms are largely based on expert consensus rather than randomized controlled trials in adult populations. Moving forward, the integration of biomarker-driven diagnostics, genetic profiling, and targeted combination therapies holds the promise of transforming adult HLH from a syndrome defined by clinical pattern recognition into a precision medicine paradigm in which treatment is guided by pathogenic mechanism. The cross-disciplinary collaborations catalyzed by the pandemic and by the growth of immunotherapy provide a strong foundation for this next phase of progress.

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
